checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. It presents the proclamation in a single-column layout with the state seal at the top, followed by the governor's name, document headers, the body of the proclamation, and finally the seal and signature at the bottom. Although navigation elements and a redundant text section have been added, the primary visual organization faithfully reflects the source document's structure.
The source document does not contain any tables, and no tables are rendered in the HTML output.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the State Seal of North Carolina at the top and the combined image of the gold embossed seal with ribbons and the Governor's signature at the bottom. These images appear correctly rendered in the HTML screenshot.

The entire body content of the proclamation is visually duplicated on the page. The document first renders the proclamation in a layout that includes embedded images (under the 'Proclamation' heading), and then repeats the entire text again in a structured text-only section below. While this is likely an intentional strategy for accessibility, it results in the identical five 'WHEREAS' paragraphs and the 'NOW, THEREFORE' statement appearing twice in the main body area of the HTML document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains a single instance of the proclamation body text starting with "WHEREAS, children’s healthy development..." and ending with "...the two hundred and forty-eighth."
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows this entire text block appearing twice: first in the section with images, and again in the text-only section starting with the heading "State of North Carolina".
